speaker: interviewer
text: --on with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want him to do this by himself. I think I should go.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no, no, now it would be-- no, no, you need-- you need it for your experience, anyway. I just want you to know that I think it can--

speaker: interviewer
text: He's very aware. I mean, he notices when I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of them do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I think everyone has-- I mean, that's how they survived. But I think there's also the added thing that you're a very attractive female who's been very nurturing, too. So there's a little bit of this, you know, wanting to please a little bit. So I'm going to-- I'm going to still work a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It's 15th of July. And it's 'My-zils.'

speaker: interviewee
text: 'My-zelles.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Meisels-- I'm having a hard time. Andrew Meisels.

speaker: interviewer
text: Andrew, Andrew, you spoke earlier about the sadistic prisoner in Auschwitz who pointed to the chimney, to the smoke, and told you that's where your parents are. But you haven't mentioned yourself at what point you became aware that your-- that your parents had perished. So can you tell us a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --little bit about--

speaker: interviewee
text: I became aware of it shortly thereafter that the man was telling the truth. As we-- as I was sent to Auschwitz as a punishment, you heard it from, from a lot of other people there, too, that that's, that's the assembly line that they are doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you meet people who were on the same transport, perhaps, with your family, with your parents? Did you meet any witnesses who said, yes--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --we saw-- we, we saw them disappear?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, you don't-- you don't meet-- those people would be dead also. You, you just wouldn't meet them. There was what they call the 'Sonderkommando.' Their job was to-- instead of shower, they got gas. And their job was to throw them in the-- in the crematorium. I only found out afterwards of how the assembly of killing went on. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So soon after that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Oh, that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --gave up hope after your arrival there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Because it sort of made sense. The stench was flesh, burning flesh, which-- it, it was such a strange stench, smell, that, that it had to be, like they said, that's burning flesh. I'm saying it calmly. But let me tell you, throughout the entire [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] prison camp that I was in, I saw sadism-- cruelty from the Germans, and anyone that had a higher rank, whether it was a prisoner himself-- but he had to show to the Germans that I'm doing what you might want me to do, and so on. I saw a lot of cruelty. I never thought that people can handle people, other people, like that with such cruelty-- and, and killings at will, shootings at will. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] By the way, my son came home a few weeks ago. He said, Dad, I went to see And he says to me, don't see it. It's not for you. Mom, don't let him go. And he walks out of the room. And I said to my wife, I said, you know I know I shouldn't see it. But I know I must see it. I have to go see it. And I went on a Sunday afternoon, figuring I'll be calm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sunday afternoon, I went with my wife. I ran out three times just to cry because I didn't want to do it inside. And the man that's selling popcorn, he saw how upset I was. And he says, would you like a glass of water? I just went yes, you know. And then he brings me a cold compress with it and-- which I thought I was very nice of him, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went back. I knew it was going to be more pain. But I had to go back. It's just one of those things. I had to see it. Just like a few years ago when the very first time they showed the on television, like Monday two hours, Tuesday two hours, and Wednesday two hours, from 9:00 to 11:00, and so on. That was with Meryl Streep and-- the original I was invited to see it in a movie before it was shown on television, for-- on, on a Tuesday for four and a half hours and on a Wednesday for four and a half hours in Century City. I went to see it. I ran out a few times just to throw up because it just affected me that much. But I went back for more pain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gross.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just had to see it. [SIGHS] And then afterwards, some of the people-- I think they were from CBS-- they asked me, what did I think of it? You know, what did you think of the program of the I said, well, you showed a lot of cruelty. Like, you showed how they herded in a bunch of people into a synagogue. And they set the synagogue on fire. And everybody inside burned. That was a cruel picture. But it was a lot worse than what you filmed here. And you didn't even film the tenth of it. It was that much worse. Well, like for example, can you give us-- and then that's when I told them the example about my friend wanting to sit down during the death march, and so on. I said, you didn't film that. In other words, they didn't film feelings of the-- of a person that survived. They didn't film that. They just filmed how cruel the Germans were. They lined up people. They made them dig their own ditches. And they shot and machine gunned them down-- fine. That's a happening that you saw. But you didn't film what did this other person feel that might have been hiding, and made it, stayed alive, and so on. And these things cannot be filmed. I know that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I'm glad you brought that up because I noticed that when you talked about those first days in Birkenau and when you were beaten, it was a very difficult moment for you. And then you stopped yourself from crying. And you even apologized. And I'm wondering what it is that you're trying so hard to, to keep from--

speaker: interviewer
text: --opening-- or this vulnerability that you seem to be hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm trying to stop myself from continuing to cry. I cry on the inside many, many times. And then, even now, I just try to think of, of other things. I like to change the subject so that I shouldn't cry. After all, I'm, I'm 68 years old. And a man of my age doesn't cry that easily.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, Andrew, if I may interject, as someone who didn't go through anything like what you've been through, I think for us to understand what you've been through, the only way for us to understand this, to know some of those feelings that you experienced, that were generated by the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we can talk about feelings. I'll tell you a little bit of a feeling, which is just an interesting thing that happened in my life, you know. This was already-- I'm in New York. I wake up in a nightmare, like two, three nights in a row. I lived with an aunt or uncle of mine who had three small children-- and nine, seven, and five, something like that. And they, they just loved me. You know, it's a, a cousin that made it through, and, and so on. And, like 2:00 in the morning, my aunt wakes me up and drags me to the shower. I'm stark naked. I, I, I slept, at that time, in the raw. Turns on the shower-- and then she tells me, you had a bad dream. It's OK. It's OK. You know, I mean, they just love me to pieces. Three-- and this, this went on several nights like that. And one day, my uncle tells me, don't go to school today-- because I started going to school. My uncle tells me, don't go to school. I want you to spend the day with me today. I want-- I told a friend of mine about you. And he, he would like to meet you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I go down-- it was Central Park West on 57th Street, somewhere around there. I go up. And I said, but Uncle, I don't-- I don't speak any English, I whispered to him. He says, don't worry because he speaks German. So we talked in German.

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle is sitting by the window, reading a paper. And he's talking to me for about an hour and a half. Pretty soon, my uncle gets up to go to the bathroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: The guy takes out $10 from his pocket. He said, listen, can you take a taxi day after tomorrow, come back, and see me without your uncle? I'd like to talk to you without your uncle.

speaker: interviewee
text: I give him back his $10. I know how to get there by bus. I lived on 90th street and Broadway. And he was on 57th and Broadway, Central Park.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I go back there on Thursday. And again, we talked for about an hour and a half. And it, it, it made me feel, somehow, a little better talking. Just to make the story short, I wound up going to this guy three times a week for nine months-- when I found out he's a psychiatrist that my uncle paid for, for me, $25 a visit because I was-- I was-- I, I was-- I didn't want to live.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: I have guilty feelings. Why did I remain alive, and not one of my brothers, or not my younger brother, one immediately younger? He was a handsome guy. Girls were hovering around him all the time. And [CRYING] I just-- I was crying all the time. And one day-- one day, his relatives came to visit us, and so on. And my uncle, he says, I want to ask you a question. How did it happen that my brother, your father, let himself be taken by the Germans, just like that? I-- my answer was-- I just started to cry. Even though I was taken from Romania, I mean, I just imagined myself-- what do you do when four Germans bust the door down with machine guns, roust everybody out? I said, what do you do? I mean, I had an uncle, as it happens, he was shot on the street because he wasn't moving fast enough for them. So what kind of a stupid question is that? I don't know if I said stupid because that's-- you don't talk to an uncle like that. That uncle, he [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] made me want to live again, you know? Actually, the psychiatrist slowly, slowly, he made me understand that life must go on. There's got to be a reason why God left you alive, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the reason is-- I showed you a picture of my family. That's my reason [LAUGHS] for-- I'm glad that I remained alive. I found a, a wife. And I, I still can't believe that I have my own two children, whom I love-- I mean, I know other people love their kids. But I'm being accused in the family-- I've never seen a crazier father than you are. And I think they're right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you think it has something to do with your losses that you're as close to your family now because--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I, I was-- I was-- I was-- I'm close with my family also. I was-- I was a good boy in my family, among the five of us, brothers and sisters. My, my mother always said that nobody helps me as much as Andy does. I mean my nickname was [NON-ENGLISH], you know. And when my aunt wrote to her from Sighet, Romania, how hard I'm working and so on, that she finally sent me to the movies, and so on, this got around. And my mother wrote me a beautiful letter that she's [CRYING] proud of me, and so on. And [LAUGHS] you made me cry after all and I'm trying not to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually, I, I didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: --it was your mother-- it was your mother's letter that you remembered. Do you remember anything in the letter that you can share?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just nice. I don't remember it word by word. It was just a nice letter, letting me know how proud she is of me. She hears nice things about me, and so on. And I'm living up to her expectations, and so on and so forth. But [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I'll probably remember other, little anecdotes as, as you will-- when you leave, and so on. But you can't remember every single little piece that you want to-- might want to tell about, and so on, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: That happens very often when people remember afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The one thing I do remember is that I have never seen such cruelty in my entire life as the German, German soldiers were. I'm not saying the German people. I can't hold everything against the people. But they were-- they had to know-- most of them had to know what's going on. I'm talking about the people that lived in those days. The ones that were born after that, you really can't hold things against them this much. But I just hope that the world is never going to let them rise again. I just had to put that in. I don't know who will see this, and so on. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I was going to ask you if you had bitterness towards the German people. But you've answered that. You've-- you're, you're putting the responsibility on the people that were involved.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have bitterness. I have a lot of bitterness against the German people, the older people, the ones that, that lived during those times. When they were merely asking, who are those people? And they were given the answer that these are criminals-- and that was an acceptable answer. They, they should have asked more and done more not to let this happen. I have never-- I mean, you can see what's going on in the world now in South Africa, and so on. They're killing people. But nothing was ever happening like this, a systematic killing factory of, of a people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How dare you come into my house-- my country and my house and drag me to your country, or to Poland somewhere, to be gassed and killed? I mean, how dare you do that? It's just-- it's mind-boggling. I mean, I'm making a speech now, and I wasn't planning to. It's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I just rebel against that. This is so unjust.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the collaborators, the people who-- obviously, the Germans couldn't have done all that by themselves. What about the people that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm faulting the Hungarian government as well. They played into the German people's hands. Romania-- at least the Romanian king-- said, you can do with the Jews whatever you want. But in this country, you cannot take them out of here. You can-- in other words, they can use-- you can use them for forced labor, to dig ditches, or whatever. Talking about digging ditches, you see, that reminds me of another episode, one time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Gleiwitz. In the camp of Gleiwitz. I don't know why, why it happened. I'm trying to remember. A German police dog bit me, right here, [POINTS] right under my knee. Because the Germans said, get him! You know, it was fun for him. Get him! So he got me. It took-- for three or four months, I still had pus in there. I finally decided I'm going to go to the hospital. They had-- we had a hospital there, inside the, the camp. So I go to the doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he says-- this looks bad, he says. And he put some medication on there. And he says, when did you get this? And I told him. Why didn't you come sooner? I says, I was afraid, you know. Finally-- and he says, keep this on. And come back next week, and I'll change the dressing, and so on. Finally, I asked him, I says, can I stay here for two or three days, just to-- just to relax, and this? He says, this is the worst place you would want to stay at. The Germans come here every single day. And they pick out the sick people. And they disappear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now he was telling-- this was a French doctor. He was telling all this to me in Yiddish. He probably saved my life. If I would've stayed there, I would not have survived. And it took for-- a long time to heal, just a long time. Because I didn't have enough food. I didn't have enough vitamins. And all this, because one guy, it was fun for him how he got-- egged the dog on me, one of those big German police dogs. [SIGHS] Why do I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who the kapos, the guards were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember some of them, their faces are right in front of me.

speaker: interviewer
text: But--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember names.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about nationalities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some of them-- the, the one that was our barrack leader-- that wasn't a Polish Jew, you know. He was a German prisoner. And he had a red sign, you know. The red sign was a political prisoner. A black one was-- one of those triangles-- like we had the Jewish Star, they had little triangles. The black was, I think, a burglar. And the-- no, the green was a burglar. And the black was a murderer, you know. And that was the worst. And this one had a, a, a red one. But he was a cruel German, just cruel. He'd just beat you for the hell of it. I mean, many times, when, when they were giving out the lunch, which was a bowl of soup. Now the bowl of soup was smelly-- dead horse meat in there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just-- you would puke today, you know, if you would just-- somebody would bring it close enough to you. But at that time, it was good food. And when they called for seconds, people ran to the guys that were handing out the seconds. But then, there were too many of them at the same time. And they started beating with those big rubber hoses. I learned not to go. I just-- I'd rather-- one of those smacks took more out of you than the additional food that you might get.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I just learned to control myself and not go for seconds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the selections in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- which selections are you talking about now, as, as we arrived?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you mentioned that one. We went over that. I mean, while you were in the camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, I remember selections. I surely do. There were some high-ranking German SS. They were walking on by with some lower-ranking people that were making notes, and whatnot. And he says, you, step over there. Another one, you, step over there. And I, I was just praying. It, it didn't happen to me, just luck. I was always trying to stay one row behind. Because there were several rows of people. I mean, it isn't that you went one by one by one. It wasn't an immediate, detailed inspection. It was just generally walking on by. And they spotted a very thin person, they just asked him to step out-- never saw him again.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned luck. Do think it was luck that you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: A great deal of it was luck. And I'll tell you why. One, one time-- when I went to this doctor, to the German doctor, and he gave me a, a bandage, and so on, and I come into work the next day to the factory, he sees the bandage. And he says, no, you, you stay in your camp today. I stayed in the camp. Two hours later, somebody, you, come over here. And they put me into a group where there was, like, 20 people. They were digging ditches. And I had to dig ditches. Here comes this prisoner-- a prison suit, but a German prisoner. He had a black thing that's-- and nobody was fast enough for him. He just kept hitting you with, with, with that big rubber hose. And I said to myself, if I have to do this for the rest of my prison life, you know, I'm not going to survive even a week. I think I did that for, like, three days. I-- every evening, when I got back to my barracks, I just collapsed in there. I was so tired. Finally, one morning, I see a group of German-- high-ranking Germans just talking to themselves. And I took up enough courage. And I walked up there. And I started telling them in German that I have been assigned accidentally to a job that I'm really not-- one doesn't need skill for it. I am more skillful to work in the factory that I have been working all this time. And it was just a mistake that-- I must have caught him in a good mood. Because he said, all right, report to your-- to your previous man. Tell them that I said you are to work over there. And that's how I wound up back in my regular job, where I was reading on the door of the wagon, what's wrong with this, and what's wrong with that, and so on. And this girl, that Polish girl, probably helped me toward saving my life. I tried to find her whereabouts after, after the war. I just couldn't. I didn't even-- didn't even remember her name, I'm sorry to say. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] these, these are-- these are things that, that saved my life, you know. And this, this, this girl, that-- she got into the habit. One morning, she just brought me a, a small tomato. I had never eaten a tomato in my life. I always hated it. This one was delicious. That was my first tomato in my whole life. And these are the little things that I remember. And I will probably remember other things once you leave. [LAUGHS] So--

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a, a very unusual experience post-Holocaust in-- when you came to New York. First of all, having extremely loving relatives who cared enough to provide--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you with a psychiatrist, which is unusual in itself. Many survivors did not have that available to them, especially from the very beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't even know what a psychiatrist is, frankly. You know, I mean, what do they do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can--

speaker: interviewee
text: And slowly, I was made to understand that life must go on. There's a reason why you were left alive, and so on and so on. And, as I said then, there is my reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think in your present-day adjustment, that it helped you a great deal to, to have been able to talk about it from the beginning with somewhere who cared and understood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably. If I would have kept everything inside of myself, I would've just burst from anger, from everything else. And this, this helped me a great deal. There's no question about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the things that you two might have talked about, any one conversation that stands out, or a piece of advice, or maybe some reflection that the two of you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I, I don't remember the specific details. But I do remember that every time I, I left there, I felt better. For some reason, I felt-- I didn't feel so tight inside. I felt better. And, frankly, I was looking forward to my next visit with him. And, and, and I said to myself, this guy's making me feel good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I-- as a matter of fact, I have-- I have it somewhere, a letter from him that he attended-- he says, you might need it sometime in the future-- that I attended his visits with him, and so on, for a period from this on-- onto that month, and so on and so. I never needed it. So I bet you, if I go through my old, old papers, I will find it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was he? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- he was an older man-- I'm looking older man. He must have been around his 50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is, like, around 1947-48?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were probably one of the first-- one of the first survivors to actually have therapy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, I don't know about that. But that's, that's a possibility, yes. But that's-- all that is-- that uncle of mine is 92 years old now. And he moved from New York to here. He lives here, maybe three or four miles from here. He just wanted to be closer to me and my cousin. And, all this time, he-- I should have mentioned a cousin of mine. We were involved coming out here together. When, when we were liberated, as a matter of fact-- I'm, I'm coming back to my liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to come back also to Dachau and Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's, that's what I-- that's what reminded me. At, at Dachau, you know, and, and the train that we were traveling for-- we were traveling for eight days and eight nights. And it turned out, all during that time, we did maybe 50 or 55 kilometers. The, the train was going this way, and then pretty soon, they came back. Which-- we didn't know that it's back. We just-- we just felt the train moving.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is right before you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were liberated--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from the train--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: --correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in, in Germany, about three kilometers from Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're-- we need to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we'll continue with the liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.
